Introduction

About Me

My name is Rehan Shah, an incoming freshman at Northeastern University pursuing a BS in Electrical and Computer Engineering. I completed an internship at Antron Inc., an engineering and manufacturing firm in Bellingham, MA, where I gained hands-on experience across a wide range of precision manufacturing processes.

I came in with a strong foundation in CAD (Fusion 360, SolidWorks), 3D printing, VEX Robotics, and CNC machining — but this was my first real exposure to shop floor manufacturing operations at a professional scale. It pushed me in ways the classroom never could.

Outside of engineering, I am a multi-sport varsity athlete in football, wrestling, and volleyball, an NHS member, and a SAT tutor. This internship added a new dimension to my development as a future engineer.

Work Assignments

Roles & Responsibilities

⚗️
Chemistry / QC

Passivation & Titration Testing

Performed chemical passivation treatments on metal components to remove free iron and form a protective oxide layer — a critical step for corrosion resistance, especially on medical-grade parts. Conducted titration tests to verify that chemical bath concentrations stayed within specification, requiring precise measurement and careful lab technique.

🔧
Machining / Finishing

Deburring & Manual Countersinking

Manually deburred machined parts using hand tools to eliminate sharp edges and metal burrs left over from CNC operations — a step essential for both safety and part function. Performed manual countersinking to prepare holes for flush fastener installation to spec.

⚙️
Machine Operation

Tumbling & Drying

Operated the industrial tumbling machine to batch-finish and smooth surface textures across large quantities of parts. Managed the drying process following tumbling cycles to ensure parts were free of moisture and ready for the next production stage.

🧪
Cleaning / Surface Prep

Acetone Cleaning & Degreasing

Cleaned components with acetone solvent to remove contaminants prior to surface treatments. Operated an industrial degreasing machine to strip manufacturing oils and residues — a non-negotiable step before inspection, especially for medical parts where contamination could compromise the final product.

🔩
Production

Parts Assembly

Assembled finished components according to engineering specifications and assembly instructions. Worked alongside experienced machinists and technicians, learning firsthand how precision tolerances translate from a CAD drawing to a real, functioning part.

What I Learned

Skills Gained

Chemical Process Knowledge

Passivation chemistry, titration methodology, bath concentration maintenance, and chemical safety protocols.

Precision Finishing

Deburring and countersinking techniques using hand tools to achieve clean, safe, spec-compliant part edges.

Machine Operation

Industrial tumbling and degreasing equipment — setup, cycle management, and quality checks at each stage.

Quality Control Mindset

Understanding why each step matters — cleanliness, consistency, and inspection before handoff to the next stage.

Shop Floor Professionalism

OSHA-aligned safety practices, communication with experienced machinists, and working within a real production schedule.

Assembly & Engineering Specs

Reading engineering documentation, understanding tolerances, and building precision components correctly to print.

Personal Narrative

Reflection

Why I chose this internship

I chose this internship at Antron Inc. because I wanted to bridge the gap between what I had learned in the classroom and what engineering actually looks like in a professional environment. Having spent years studying CAD, working with 3D printers, and competing in robotics, I felt confident in design and theory — but I recognized that a well-rounded engineer needs to understand how parts are actually made, not just modeled.

Antron's focus on precision manufacturing, including medical-grade components, presented an opportunity to see engineering standards applied at the highest level. Working locally in Bellingham also allowed me to fully commit to the experience while still being close to home as I prepared for college.

Expectations vs. reality

Going into this internship, I was hoping to land in a more design-focused role — spending time on CAD, working on product concepts, or contributing to engineering drawings. My background in Fusion 360 and SolidWorks led me to expect that kind of work. The reality was different: I spent most of my time on the shop floor performing hands-on manufacturing tasks — deburring, tumbling, degreasing, passivating, and assembling parts.

At first, that surprised me. But it quickly became clear that this was actually more valuable than I anticipated. Understanding why a part needs to be deburred before passivation, or why chemical bath concentrations have to be precisely controlled, gave me context I never could have gotten from a screen. I left with a much deeper appreciation for the gap between a design and a finished part — and that understanding will make me a better engineer at Northeastern and beyond.

Three things I learned

1. Titration is not just a classroom concept. In high school chemistry, titration was something I memorized for a test and moved on from. At Antron, it was a real quality control procedure I performed regularly to verify that the passivation bath was at the correct concentration. Getting it wrong could mean a batch of parts failing corrosion resistance — a significant issue when those parts are destined for medical devices. Seeing the real-world stakes made the chemistry click in a way it never had before.

2. Cleanliness standards in manufacturing are extreme — especially in medical. I knew that manufacturing required attention to detail, but I underestimated how serious contamination control is at a shop working on medical components. Many parts could not be touched with bare hands due to the oils and residue skin contact leaves behind. The degreasing and acetone cleaning steps weren't just routine — they were critical to product integrity. Following those protocols consistently required discipline and a shift in how I approached every task.

3. Every step in the process has engineering logic behind it. Before this internship, I thought of manufacturing operations like deburring or tumbling as simple, almost mechanical tasks. I quickly learned that each step exists for a specific engineering reason — deburring removes stress concentration points that could cause fatigue failure; tumbling achieves surface roughness targets that affect fit and coating adhesion. Understanding the "why" behind each process made me far more engaged and effective on the floor.

Personal growth

The most significant area of personal growth I experienced was learning to take ownership of repetitive, precision-driven work without losing focus or cutting corners. As an athlete, I'm used to high-intensity moments with clear outcomes — a play, a match, a score. Manufacturing is different. Many of the tasks I performed required sustained attention over long periods, where a single lapse in care could mean an entire batch needed to be redone or rejected.

That taught me something important about professionalism: showing up fully, every day, even when the work is unglamorous. I also grew in my ability to communicate with experienced machinists and technicians who had decades of knowledge I didn't have. Learning to ask the right questions, listen carefully, and apply feedback quickly made me more effective and earned me more responsibility over time. Those are skills I'll carry directly into my engineering career.
